raw_transcript.text:
[Music] on this video we're learning the 
basics of cinematography and that starts right

[Music] now hey guys we're back Ryan here 
with Camp films I hope you guys are doing

fantastic today it's a beautiful day here 
in North Carolina and today we are talking

about cinematography Basics cinematography is 
one of the most important aspects of the film

making process cinematography is one of the 
things that I try to work on the most every

time I make a new film I try to improve learn 
new things learn new techniques and while I'm

still learning and improving on my cinematography 
all the time I thought today we would take a look

at the basics of cinematography so first off what 
is cinematography cinematography basically is the

art of visual storytelling its main pillars are 
camera work lighting composition movement color

and like I said it's a fundamental aspect of film 
making and video production so let's take a look

at every core aspect of cinematography starting 
with camera work the camera of course is going

to be your primary tool in cinematography and 
whether you're using a DSLR your mobile phone

or a cinema camera understanding your device and 
your tool is very crucial to your success as a

cinematographer one of the most important things 
you can learn about controlling your camera is how

to manipulate the Holy Trinity of exposure and 
that's going to be your aperture shutter speed

and ISO settings understanding the ins and outs 
of the camera that you're shooting with is one of

the most basic steps that we can take to improve 
our cinematography study the documentation for

your camera to learn how to best set it up for 
film making watch YouTube videos dedicated to

setting up your camera to get the best out of 
your camera when shooting films and video one

thing I do to improve consist consistency and 
ease of use is to set up custom templates on

my camera for when I'm shooting films and video 
that way once you find something that works and

looks the best you can easily switch to it at any 
time by bringing up your presets but this is just

the start as far as camera work is concerned 
learning about the different lenses we can use

is also an important step different lenses are 
better for different types of shots learning

basic camera shots and camera angles is also an 
important step we can take and as I mentioned

before learning the Holy Trinity of exposure is an 
important step you can take to improving each shot

learn your camera and learn how to manipulate the 
iso aperture and shutter speeds if you would like

to learn more about the Holy Trinity of exposure 
I have a dedicated video for each aspect here on

the channel I'll put a link to those in the 
description [Music] below next let's let's

talk about composition composition is basically 
how you arrange things in the frame the rule of

thirds leading lines and framing within the frame 
learning these techniques what they mean and how

to use them is going to lead your viewer's eye to 
the subject you want them to be looking at within

the frame let's take a look at some examples of 
good and bad composition camera composition is

crucial in visual storytelling as it affects how 
the audience perceives and interprets the scene

here are some examples of good and bad camera 
compositions let's start with the good first

the rule of thirds placing the subject off center 
at one of the intersections of the dividing lines

in a 3X3 grid this creates a more aesthetically 
pleasing and balanced image leading lines using

natural lines in the scene like roads fences or 
architectural elements to lead the viewer's eye

towards the main subject depth creating a sense 
of depth by having objects in the foreground

mid-ground and background this is usually 
called creating depth of field where you

have an INF focused character in The foreground 
and an out of focus background creating a sense

of three-dimensional space Symmetry and patterns 
using symmetry or repeating patterns for visually

striking composition frame within a frame 
using elements within the scene to create

a natural frame around the subject such as 
a character being viewed through a doorway

focusing the viewer's attention now let's 
take a look at some examples of bad camera

composition poor framing subjects are awkwardly 
cut off or important elements are partially out

of the frame lack of head room or lead room too 
little or too much space above a subject's head

or not enough space in the direction a subject is 
looking or moving to Flat compositions composition

that lacks depth with everything in focus focus 
and no clear distinction between foreground and

background a distracting background elements in 
the background distract from the main subject

like a busy or cluttered background that 
takes attention away from the subject and

an unbalanced composition the image feels visually 
unbalanced with too much weight on one side of the

frame understanding and applying good composition 
techniques can significantly enhance the visual

impact of your scenes while poor composition can 
extract or confuse the viewer detracting from your

storytelling up next is a big one and that is 
lighting lighting is everything it's going to

shape the mood the atmosphere and the realism of 
your scene since we're going over the basics the

three-point lighting setup is a great place to 
start let's take a look at what it is and how we

can use it in our films a great place to start 
when you're first learning about lighting is

the three-point lighting setup up this technique 
uses three lights to fully illuminate a subject

in a three-dimensional space and is known 
for its versatility an ability to create a

professional look here's a brief explanation of 
each component and how to set it up first we'll

start with the key light this is the main light in 
this setup and it's usually the strongest and has

the most influence on the look of the scene it's 
used to highlight the form and dimensions of the

subject place the key light to one side of the 
camera about 45° from it and a bit higher than

eye level the angle can be adjusted to control the 
shadows and highlights thereby creating different

moods and styles according to what kind of look 
you're going for next we have the fill light the

fillite balances the key light by Illuminating 
the Shadows created by the key light it's softer

and less intense than the key light position 
The Fill light on the opposite side of the key

light at about the same angle The Fill should be 
lessened intense so either move it further away

from the subject or use a dimmer light the goal 
is to soften the Shadows without eliminating them

completely you can sometimes even get away with 
using a bounce instead of an actual light to fill

in the subject on this side and last we have the 
back light also known as a rim light or a hair

light this light is placed behind the subject 
and it helps to separate the subject from the

background adding depth and dimension to the shot 
place the backlight behind the subject opposite

the camera it should be higher than the subject 
pointing downward this creates a slight rim of

light around the edges of the subject helping 
them stand out from the background be sure to

experiment with the positioning and intensity 
of each light to achieve the desired effect use

diffusers or bounce the light off surfaces to 
create softer shadows and reduce harshness the

three-point lighting setup is adaptable you can 
modify it based on the specific requirements of

your shoot such as the subject's features 
the camera angle and the mood you want to

convey let's talk about using movement in our 
cinematography how your camera moves is almost

as important as what you're capturing a pan 
tilt or Dolly shot can reveal details create

tension and evoke emotions but remember movement 
should always serve the story let's take a look

at some different kinds of camera movement we 
can use in our cinematography in cinematography

camera movement is a powerful tool to tell your 
story convey emotions and guide the audience's

attention here are some of the most common types 
of camera movement to help get you started first

we have the pan shot in a pan shot the camera is 
stationary but rotates on its axis horizontally

it moves from left to right or right to left 
painting is often used to follow a moving subject

or to reveal the envir environment horizontally 
up next we have the Tilt similar to panning but

the camera moves vertically up or down while 
remaining fixed in its position tilting is

used to reveal elements vertically such as tall 
buildings or a character looking up or down next

we have the dolly or tracking shot in a dolly 
shot the camera physically moves towards or

away from the subject or it moves alongside the 
subject this movement can create a sense of depth

and is often used for following a character or 
moving through a physical space up next we have

the zoom shot unlike Dolly shots zooming doesn't 
involve moving the camera instead the lens focal

length is changed to make the subject appear 
closer or further away zooms can be used for

dramatic effect to focus on a specific detail 
or to transition between scenes next we have

the crane or the jib shot this is when the 
camera is mounted on a crane or jib and moves

vertically and sometimes horizontally this is 
used to achieve high angle shots or to move up

and down over top of objects providing a bird's 
eye view or a sweeping shot of the environment

and we have the handheld shot this is when the 
camera is held in the camera operator's hands

handheld shots often result in shaky footage 
and has more of a documentary style feel which

is used to create a sense of realism or 
urgency and that's not all we still have

steady cam shots drone shots and there's even 
more but this should be enough to help get you

started with your cinematography Journey each of 
these movements can be used in various creative

ways to enhance your storytelling creating 
mood and engaging your audience the choice

of movement often depends on the narrative needs 
the style of the film and the director's overall

vision let's talk about the power of color in 
our cinematography color can tell the viewer

how to feel without a single word color grading 
can enhance your narrative but it all starts with

good white balance and exposure let's go over some 
color grading Basics to get us off to a good start

before we talk about color we need to understand 
white balance white balance is a fundamental

Concept in photography and videography that 
affects the color accuracy of your images it's

about adjusting colors so that they appear more 
natural compensating for the color temperature

or different light sources here's a breakdown of 
the basics color temperature is measured in kelvin

it refers to the warmth or coolness of a light 
source lower temperatures around 1,000 to 3,000

are warmer yellowish or orange like candle light 
higher temperatures above 5,000k are cool blue

like a cloudy Sky different light sources like 
sunlight fluorescent light tungsten bulbs and LED

lights all have different color temperatures that 
you need to account for the reason it's important

is that without correct white balance photos can 
have an unrealistic Color cast too blue or too

orange making them look unnatural ensuring color 
consistency across different shots is especially

important in professional settings like product 
photography or film making when you go to set your

white balance most cameras have an auto white 
balance setting where the camera automatically

adjusts the white balance good for General use 
but may not always be completely accurate you

can also try to use presets with your white 
balance most cameras typically offer presets

for different lighting conditions like cloudy days 
uh daylight indoor lighting fluorescent lighting

Etc these presets are based on average color 
temperatures for those light sources you can

also set your custom white balance for precise 
control you can set the white balance manually

this is done by photographing a white or neutral 
gray card under the lighting you'll be using and

setting the image as the reference for your white 
balance and some cameras also have a Kelvin mode

some Advanced cameras allow you to set the color 
temperature directly in kelvin giving you full

control over your white balance be sure to test 
and adjust and always test your white balance

settings and adjust as needed especially in mixed 
lighting conditions you can also shoot in raw if

you're unsure shooting in raw allows you to 
adjust white balance during postprocessing

without quality loss for critical work you can 
use a gray card to set a custom white balance for

accurate color reproduction be aware of changing 
light conditions especially when shooting Outdoors

understanding and correctly setting white balance 
is crucial for producing images that accurately

reflect the scene's colors enhancing the overall 
quality and professionalism of your work now that

we know the importance of white balance and 
how to set it up on our cameras now it's time

to delve into color color in cinematography is 
a powerful storytelling tool influencing mood

atmosphere and even the audience's emotional 
response understanding the basics of color

involves several key aspects first let's talk 
about color theory Hue saturation and brightness

hue refers to the color itself red blue green 
Etc saturation is the intensity or purity of the

color and brightness or the value refers to how 
light or dark the color is complimentary colors

opposites each other on the color wheel create 
contrast and vibrancy colors that are next to

each other on the color wheel offer Harmony and 
are pleasing to the eye different colors evoke

different emotions for example blue can feel calm 
and Serene while red can signify passion or Danger

let's talk about color temperature as mentioned 
earlier color temperature in film affects the

mood and atmosphere warmer tones a lower Kelvin 
often convey warmth and comfort while cooler tones

higher Kelvin can feel sterile or distant now 
let's talk about color grading and correction

color correction is adjusting colors to ensure 
they are realistic and consistent across all of

your shots this step is essential for maintaining 
continuity color grading on the other hand is the

creative process of altering and enhancing the 
color of a motion picture it's used to create

a visual tone and mood here are a few color 
schemes that we can use in our cinematography

monochromatic which uses variations in lightness 
and saturation of a single color this can create a

very focused and cohesive look complimentary which 
utilizes colors opposite of each other on the

color wheel it's great for creating contrast and 
highlighting elements and analogous which involves

colors that are next to each other on the color 
wheel this scheme is often found in nature and is

harmonious and pleasing to the eye here are some 
ways that we can use color to help tell our story

it can be used in character development colors 
can reflect a character's Journey or changes

in mood and personality it can create symbolism 
certain colors can symbolize different themes or

ideas like green for Envy or growth and colors 
can help set the scene color palettes can help

establish the setting and the time period in 
your film here are some practical applications

for using color in your films and cinematography 
lighting gels and filters can be used on lights

to add color to a scene they can be used in your 
production design wardrobe set design and props

are chosen for their color to contribute to the 
overall palette of your scenes and color can

be used in post- production digital tools allow 
for precise control over the colors in the final

product maintaining a consistent color palette 
throughout the film helps in creating a cohesive

visual style this consists Y is key in making the 
film visually compelling and narratively coherent

to summarize color in cinematography is not just 
about making the film look good it's an essential

element of visual storytelling it requires a 
thoughtful approach from pre-production planning

to post-production finishing to effectively 
convey the story's emotional and thematic

undertones now that we know know the basics of 
cinematography it's time to put all of these

things into action and practice our craft 
three simple ways that you can practice

your cinematography are with still life portraits 
and action scenes photography is a great place to

start try different compositions lighting setups 
and movement one of the most useful ways I found

in improving my cinematography and getting some 
good practice is to watch some of my favorite

films and try to recreate scenes from those films 
you'll be surprised that with a little trial and

error can help you accomplish cinematography 
is a journey that we are all on as filmmakers

and we've all just taken the first step remember 
that every great cinematographer started with the

basics that you learned here today so grab your 
camera start shooting and most importantly have

fun thank you guys so much for watching this video 
we have some new merch in the store so if you're

interested in that kind of stuff go check that 
out with a link below my filmmakers field guide

just hit the online store it's an incredible 
resource source of cheat sheetss and forms for

filmmakers please be sure to check it out and 
help support the channel I hope you found this

video very beneficial my name is Ryan this is 
Camp films and I will see you on the next video
[Music] bye-bye on
